The authors introduce DONUT (a synthetic benchmark with controlled topological complexity) and FILTR (Filtration Transformer), a learnable framework that predicts persistence diagrams from frozen encoders using a transformer decoder for set prediction. Results show existing encoders retain limited global topological signals, but FILTR can approximate persistence diagrams from these features, enabling data-driven extraction of topological descriptors from raw point clouds.
AI.motivation: To determine whether topological information (persistence diagrams) can be derived from features produced by pretrained 3D point cloud encoders, bridging geometric/semantic representation learning with topological data analysis.
AI.method: The authors introduce DONUT (synthetic benchmark with controlled topological complexity) and propose FILTR (Filtration Transformer), a transformer decoder that treats persistence diagram generation as a set prediction task, operating directly on frozen encoder features.
AI.result: Existing pretrained 3D encoders retain only limited global topological signals. However, FILTR can successfully leverage these encoder features to approximate persistence diagrams.
AI.conclusion: This work enables, for the first time, data-driven extraction of persistence diagrams from raw point clouds via an efficient learnable feed-forward mechanism, suggesting potential for integrating topological reasoning into learned 3D representations.

title: Flow4DGS-SLAM: Optical Flow-Guided 4D Gaussian Splatting SLAM
summary: Handling the dynamic environments is a significant research challenge in Visual Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM). Recent research combines 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) with SLAM to achieve both robust camera pose estimation and photorealistic renderings. However, using SLAM to efficiently reconstruct both static and dynamic regions remains challenging. In this work, we propose an efficient framework for dynamic 3DGS SLAM guided by optical flow. Using the input depth and prior optical flow, we first propose a category-agnostic motion mask generation strategy by fitting a camera ego-motion model to decompose the optical flow. This module separates dynamic and static Gaussians and simultaneously provides flow-guided camera pose initialization. We boost the training speed of dynamic 3DGS by explicitly modeling their temporal centers at keyframes. These centers are propagated using 3D scene flow priors and are dynamically initialized with an adaptive insertion strategy. Alongside this, we model the temporal opacity and rotation using a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) to adaptively learn the complex dynamics. The empirical results demonstrate our state-of-the-art performance in tracking, dynamic reconstruction, and training efficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an efficient dynamic 3DGS SLAM framework guided by optical flow, using motion mask generation via camera ego-motion decomposition, temporal center propagation with 3D scene flow priors, and GMM-based temporal opacity/rotation modeling, achieving state-of-the-art performance in tracking, dynamic reconstruction, and training efficiency.
AI.motivation: Handling dynamic environments remains a significant challenge in visual SLAM. While recent 3DGS+SLAM methods achieve robust pose estimation and photorealistic rendering, efficiently reconstructing both static and dynamic regions is still difficult.
AI.method: 1) Category-agnostic motion mask generation using camera ego-motion model fitting on optical flow. 2) Explicit temporal center modeling at keyframes with 3D scene flow priors and adaptive insertion. 3) GMM-based temporal opacity and rotation modeling for complex dynamics.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance in tracking accuracy, dynamic reconstruction quality, and training efficiency compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed optical-flow-guided framework effectively handles dynamic scenes in 3DGS SLAM, achieving superior performance while maintaining training efficiency.

title: PoseFM: Relative Camera Pose Estimation Through Flow Matching
summary: Monocular visual odometry (VO) is a fundamental computer vision problem with applications in autonomous navigation, augmented reality and more. While deep learning-based methods have recently shown superior accuracy compared to traditional geometric pipelines, particularly in environments where handcrafted features struggle due to poor structure or lighting conditions, most rely on deterministic regression, which lacks the uncertainty awareness required for robust applications. We propose PoseFM, the first framework to reformulate monocular frame-to-frame VO as a generative task using Flow Matching (FM). By leveraging FM, we model camera motion as a distribution rather than a point estimate, learning to transform noise into realistic pose predictions via continuous-time ODEs. This approach provides a principled mechanism for uncertainty estimation and enables robust motion inference under challenging visual conditions. In our evaluations, PoseFM achieves strong performance on TartanAir, KITTI and TUM-RGBD benchmarks, achieving the lowest absolute trajectory error (ATE) on some of the trajectories and overall being competitive with the best frame-to-frame monocular VO methods. Code and model checkpoints will be made available at https://github.com/helsinki-sda-group/posefm.

AI.tldr: PoseFM is the first framework to reformulate monocular frame-to-frame visual odometry as a generative task using Flow Matching, modeling camera motion as a distribution rather than a point estimate, achieving competitive results on TartanAir, KITTI, and TUM-RGBD benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning-based monocular VO methods rely on deterministic regression, lacking uncertainty awareness required for robust applications, especially in environments with poor structure or lighting where traditional features struggle.
AI.method: PoseFM uses Flow Matching (FM) to model camera motion as a distribution, learning to transform noise into realistic pose predictions via continuous-time ODEs, enabling uncertainty estimation and robust motion inference.
AI.result: Achieves the lowest absolute trajectory error (ATE) on some trajectories and is competitive with the best frame-to-frame monocular VO methods on TartanAir, KITTI, and TUM-RGBD benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: PoseFM demonstrates that generative modeling via Flow Matching is a promising direction for monocular VO, providing uncertainty estimation while maintaining competitive accuracy.

title: One Shot Learning for Edge Detection on Point Clouds
comment: 17 pages, 14 figures. Published in IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
summary: Each scanner possesses its unique characteristics and exhibits its distinct sampling error distribution. Training a network on a dataset that includes data collected from different scanners is less effective than training it on data specific to a single scanner. Therefore, we present a novel one-shot learning method allowing for edge extraction on point clouds, by learning the specific data distribution of the target point cloud, and thus achieve superior results compared to networks that were trained on general data distributions. More specifically, we present how to train a lightweight network named OSFENet (One-Shot edge Feature Extraction Network), by designing a filtered-KNN-based surface patch representation that supports a one-shot learning framework. Additionally, we introduce an RBF_DoS module, which integrates Radial Basis Function-based Descriptor of the Surface patch, highly beneficial for the edge extraction on point clouds. The advantage of the proposed OSFENet is demonstrated through comparative analyses against 7 baselines on the ABC dataset, and its practical utility is validated by results across diverse real-scanned datasets, including indoor scenes like S3DIS dataset, and outdoor scenes such as the Semantic3D dataset and UrbanBIS dataset.

AI.tldr: The paper presents OSFENet, a one-shot learning method for edge extraction on point clouds that adapts to the specific data distribution of a target scanner, outperforming general-trained networks across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Different scanners have unique sampling error distributions, making general-trained networks less effective than scanner-specific ones. The authors aim to achieve superior edge extraction by learning the specific data distribution of a target point cloud.
AI.method: The method proposes OSFENet (One-Shot edge Feature Extraction Network) with two key components: (1) a filtered-KNN-based surface patch representation supporting one-shot learning, and (2) an RBF_DoS module integrating Radial Basis Function-based Descriptor of the Surface patch.
AI.result: Compared against 7 baselines on ABC dataset, OSFENet shows advantage. Practical utility validated on diverse real-scanned datasets: indoor (S3DIS) and outdoor (Semantic3D, UrbanBIS).
AI.conclusion: One-shot learning tailored to a specific scanner's data distribution yields better edge extraction on point clouds than networks trained on general data distributions.

title: Efficient Diffusion Distillation via Embedding Loss
summary: Recent advances in distilling expensive diffusion models into efficient few-step generators show significant promise. However, these methods typically demand substantial computational resources and extended training periods, limiting accessibility for resource-constrained researchers, and existing supplementary loss functions have notable limitations. Regression loss requires pre-generating large datasets before training and limits the student model to the teacher's performance, while GAN-based losses suffer from training instability and require careful tuning. In this paper, we propose Embedding Loss (EL), a novel supplementary loss function that complements existing diffusion distillation methods to enhance generation quality and accelerate training with smaller batch sizes. Leveraging feature embeddings from a diverse set of randomly initialized networks, EL effectively aligns the feature distributions between the distilled few-step generator and the original data. By computing Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) in the embedded feature space, EL ensures robust distribution matching, thereby preserving sample fidelity and diversity during distillation. Within distribution matching distillation frameworks, EL demonstrates strong empirical performance for one-step generators. On the CIFAR-10 dataset, our approach achieves state-of-the-art FID values of 1.475 for unconditional generation and 1.380 for conditional generation. Beyond CIFAR-10, we further validate EL across multiple benchmarks and distillation methods, including ImageNet, AFHQ-v2, and FFHQ datasets, using DMD, DI, and CM distillation frameworks, demonstrating consistent improvements over existing one-step distillation methods. Our method also reduces training iterations by up to 80%, offering a more practical and scalable solution for deploying diffusion-based generative models in resource-constrained environments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Embedding Loss (EL) as a novel supplementary loss function for diffusion model distillation that uses random network feature embeddings with MMD to align distributions, achieving better generation quality with up to 80% fewer training iterations and SOTA FID scores on CIFAR-10 (1.475 unconditional, 1.380 conditional), while avoiding issues like regression loss pre-generation requirements and GAN training instability.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion distillation methods require massive computational resources and extended training. Current supplementary losses have shortcomings: regression loss needs pre-generated datasets and caps student performance at teacher level; GAN losses suffer from training instability. There's a need for a more efficient, stable, and accessible loss function.
AI.method: Proposes Embedding Loss (EL) that uses feature embeddings from diverse randomly initialized networks, computes Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) in this embedded feature space to align distributions between the distilled few-step generator and original data.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA FID of 1.475 (unconditional) and 1.380 (conditional) on CIFAR-10. Validated across multiple benchmarks (ImageNet, AFHQ-v2, FFHQ) and distillation frameworks (DMD, DI, CM). Reduces training iterations by up to 80%.
AI.conclusion: EL provides a practical, stable, and efficient supplementary loss that democratizes diffusion model distillation by significantly reducing computational requirements while improving generation quality, making it suitable for resource-constrained settings.

title: HFS-TriNet: A Three-Branch Collaborative Feature Learning Network for Prostate Cancer Classification from TRUS Videos
summary: Transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) imaging is a cost-effective and non-invasive modality widely used in the diagnosis of prostate cancer. The computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) relying on TRUS images has been extensively investigated recently. Compared to static images, TRUS video provides richer spatial-temporal information, which make it a promising alternative for improving the accuracy and robustness of CAD systems. However, TRUS video analysis also introduces new challenges. These include information redundancy, which increases computational costs; high intra- and inter-class similarity, which complicates feature extraction; and a low signal-to-noise ratio, which hinders the identification of clinically relevant information. To address these problems, we propose a heuristic frame selection (HFS) and a three-branch collaborative feature learning network (HFS-TriNet) for prostate cancer classification from TRUS videos. Specifically, selecting a clip of video frames at intervals for training can mitigate redundancy. The HFS strategy dynamically initializes the starting point of each training clip, which ensures that the sampled clips span the entire video sequence. For better feature extraction, besides a regular ResNet50 branch, we also utilize 1) a large model branch based a pre-trained medical segment anything model (SAM) to extract deep features of each frame and a normalization-based attention module to explore the temporal consistency; and 2) a wavelet transform convolutional residual (WTCR) branch that extracts lesion edge information in the high-frequency domain and performs denoising in the low-frequency domain.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes HFS-TriNet, a three-branch network with heuristic frame selection for prostate cancer classification from TRUS videos, addressing redundancy, high similarity, and low signal-to-noise ratio challenges.
AI.motivation: To improve prostate cancer diagnosis from TRUS videos by leveraging spatial-temporal information, while overcoming challenges of information redundancy, high intra/inter-class similarity, and low signal-to-noise ratio.
AI.method: The method consists of: (1) Heuristic Frame Selection (HFS) to dynamically sample video clips; (2) A three-branch network (TriNet) with a regular ResNet50 branch, a large model branch using pre-trained SAM with normalization-based attention for temporal consistency, and a Wavelet Transform Convolutional Residual (WTCR) branch for edge extraction and denoising.
AI.result: Not explicitly stated in the abstract; results need to be looked up in the full paper.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HFS-TriNet effectively addresses redundancy, similarity, and noise issues for TRUS video-based prostate cancer classification.

title: Region Matters: Efficient and Reliable Region-Aware Visual Place Recognition
summary: Visual Place Recognition (VPR) determines a query image's geographic location by matching it against geotagged databases. However, existing methods struggle with perceptual aliasing caused by irrelevant regions and inefficient re-ranking due to rigid candidate scheduling. To address these issues, we introduce FoL++, a method combining robust discriminative region modeling with adaptive re-ranking. Specifically, we propose a Reliability Estimation Branch to generate spatial reliability maps that explicitly model occlusion resistance. This representation is further optimized by two spatial alignment losses (SAL and SCEL) to effectively align features and highlight salient regions. For weakly supervised learning without manual annotations, a pseudo-correspondence strategy generates dense local feature supervision directly from aggregation clusters. Our Adaptive Candidate Scheduler dynamically resizes candidate pools based on global similarity. By weighting local matches by reliability and adaptively fusing global and local evidence, FoL++ surpasses traditional independent matching systems. Extensive experiments across seven benchmarks demonstrate that FoL++ achieves state-of-the-art performance with a lightweight memory footprint, improving inference speed by 40% over FoL. Code and models will be released (and merged with FoL) at https://github.com/chenshunpeng/FoL.

AI.tldr: FoL++ improves Visual Place Recognition by using reliability-based spatial feature modeling and an adaptive candidate scheduler, achieving SOTA performance with 40% faster inference than FoL.
AI.motivation: Existing VPR methods suffer from perceptual aliasing due to irrelevant regions and inefficient re-ranking caused by rigid candidate scheduling.
AI.method: Proposes (1) Reliability Estimation Branch for spatial reliability maps, (2) spatial alignment losses (SAL & SCEL) for feature alignment, (3) pseudo-correspondence strategy for weakly-supervised local feature learning, and (4) Adaptive Candidate Scheduler for dynamic pool resizing.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across seven benchmarks with lightweight memory and 40% inference speed improvement over FoL.
AI.conclusion: FoL++ effectively addresses perceptual aliasing and re-ranking inefficiency, outperforming traditional independent matching systems.

title: SpaMEM: Benchmarking Dynamic Spatial Reasoning via Perception-Memory Integration in Embodied Environments
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have advanced static visual--spatial reasoning, yet they often fail to preserve long-horizon spatial coherence in embodied settings where beliefs must be continuously revised from egocentric observations under environmental change. We introduce SpaMEM (Spatial Memory from Action Sequences), a large-scale diagnostic benchmark that isolates the mechanics of spatial belief evolution via action-conditioned scene transformations (spawn, place, remove) over long interaction horizons. SpaMEM is built on a physically grounded dataset with 10,601,392 high-fidelity images across four modalities (RGB, depth, instance, semantic segmentation), collected from 25,000+ interaction sequences in 1,000 procedurally generated houses. We formalize embodied spatial reasoning as a three-level hierarchy with 15 diagnostic tasks: Level 1 measures atomic spatial perception from single observations; Level 2 probes temporal reasoning with oracle textual state histories to factor out perceptual noise; and Level 3 requires end-to-end belief maintenance from raw visual streams under the same task dimensions. We further evaluate both short-term (step-wise) updates and long-term (episodic) reconstruction. Benchmarking representative open-source VLM families reveals a consistent stacked bottleneck: coordinate-consistent grounding remains a hard ceiling, and the sharp collapse from Level 2 to Level 3 exposes a pronounced symbolic scaffolding dependency, where models succeed with text-based bookkeeping but struggle to sustain robust visual memory. SpaMEM provides a granular diagnostic standard and motivates explicit mechanisms for state representation, belief revision, and long-horizon episodic integration.

AI.tldr: SpaMEM is a large-scale diagnostic benchmark (10.6M images across 25K+ interaction sequences) that isolates and evaluates spatial belief evolution in embodied MLLMs through action-conditioned scene transformations, revealing a stacked bottleneck where coordinate grounding fails and visual memory sharply degrades compared to text-based reasoning.
AI.motivation: MLLMs excel at static visual-spatial reasoning but fail to maintain long-horizon spatial coherence in embodied settings where beliefs must be continuously revised from egocentric observations under environmental change; existing benchmarks lack fine-grained diagnosis of this failure.
AI.method: Constructs SpaMEM with 10.6M images (RGB, depth, instance, semantic segmentation) from 25K+ interaction sequences in 1,000 procedural houses. Defines a 3-level hierarchical benchmark (atomic perception → temporal reasoning with oracle text → end-to-end visual belief maintenance) with 15 diagnostic tasks, measuring short-term step-wise and long-term episodic reconstruction.
AI.result: Open-source VLMs show a consistent stacked bottleneck: coordinate-consistent grounding is a hard ceiling; performance sharply collapses from Level 2 (text-based bookkeeping) to Level 3 (raw visual memory), exposing a symbolic scaffolding dependency.
AI.conclusion: SpaMEM provides a granular diagnostic standard for embodied spatial reasoning and motivates explicit mechanisms for state representation, belief revision, and long-horizon episodic integration.

title: NRGS: Neural Regularization for Robust 3D Semantic Gaussian Splatting
summary: We propose a neural regularization method that refines the noisy 3D semantic field produced by lifting multi-view inconsistent 2D features, in order to obtain an accurate and robust 3D semantic Gaussian Splatting. The 2D features extracted from vision foundation models suffer from multi-view inconsistency due to a lack of cross-view constraints. Lifting these inconsistent features directly into 3D Gaussians results in a noisy semantic field, which degrades the performance of downstream tasks. Previous methods either focus on obtaining consistent multi-view features in the preprocessing stage or aim to mitigate noise through improved optimization strategies, often at the cost of increased preprocessing time or expensive computational overhead. In contrast, we introduce a variance-aware conditional MLP that operates directly on the 3D Gaussians, leveraging their geometric and appearance attributes to correct semantic errors in 3D space. Experiments on different datasets show that our method enhances the accuracy of lifted semantics, providing an efficient and effective approach to robust 3D semantic Gaussian Splatting.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a variance-aware conditional MLP that directly refines noisy 3D semantic fields in Gaussian Splatting by correcting semantic errors caused by lifting multi-view inconsistent 2D features, improving accuracy without costly preprocessing.
AI.motivation: Multi-view inconsistent 2D features from vision foundation models, when lifted into 3D Gaussians, produce noisy semantic fields that degrade downstream task performance. Existing solutions either add costly preprocessing for consistency or use expensive optimization strategies.
AI.method: A variance-aware conditional MLP operates directly on 3D Gaussians, using their geometric and appearance attributes to correct semantic errors in 3D space, without requiring multi-view consistency preprocessing.
AI.result: Experiments on different datasets show enhanced accuracy of lifted semantics, demonstrating an efficient and effective approach to robust 3D semantic Gaussian Splatting.
AI.conclusion: The proposed regularization method provides an efficient alternative to preprocessing-based or optimization-heavy approaches for achieving accurate 3D semantic fields from inconsistent 2D features.

authors: Wil M. P. van der Aalst
title: All Eyes on the Workflow: Automated and Efficient Event Discovery from Video Streams
summary: Disciplines such as business process management and process mining aid organizations by discovering insights about processes on the basis of recorded event data. However, an obstacle to process analysis is data multi-modality: for instance, data in video form are not directly interpretable as events. In this work, we present SnapLog, an approach to extract event data from videos by converting frames to feature vectors using image embeddings and performing temporal segmentation through frame-wise similarity matrices. A generalized few-shot classification is then used to assign labels to the video segments, yielding labeled, timestamped sub-sequences of frames that are interpretable as events. Conventional process mining techniques can be used to analyze the resulting data. We show that our approach produces logs that accurately reflect the process in the videos.

AI.tldr: SnapLog extracts event data from videos using image embeddings and few-shot classification, enabling process mining on video data.
AI.motivation: Data multi-modality (e.g., video data) is an obstacle to process analysis because it is not directly interpretable as events.
AI.method: Converts video frames to feature vectors via image embeddings, performs temporal segmentation using similarity matrices, and applies generalized few-shot classification to assign labels to segments.
AI.result: The approach produces logs that accurately reflect the processes shown in videos.
AI.conclusion: SnapLog enables conventional process mining techniques to be applied to video data by converting them into interpretable event logs.

title: Contrastive Semantic Projection: Faithful Neuron Labeling with Contrastive Examples
summary: Neuron labeling assigns textual descriptions to internal units of deep networks. Existing approaches typically rely on highly activating examples, often yielding broad or misleading labels by focusing on dominant but incidental visual factors. Prior work such as FALCON introduced contrastive examples -- inputs that are semantically similar to activating examples but elicit low activations -- to sharpen explanations, but it primarily addresses subspace-level interpretability rather than scalable neuron-level labeling. We revisit contrastive explanations for neuron-level labeling in two stages: (1) candidate label generation with vision language models (VLMs) and (2) label assignment with CLIP-like encoders. First, we show that providing contrastive image sets to VLMs yields candidate labels that are more specific and more faithful. Second, we introduce Contrastive Semantic Projection (CSP), an extension of SemanticLens that incorporates contrastive examples directly into its CLIP-based scoring and selection pipeline. Across extensive experiments and a case study on melanoma detection, contrastive labeling improves both faithfulness and semantic granularity over state-of-the-art baselines. Our results demonstrate that contrastive examples are a simple yet powerful and currently underutilized component of neuron labeling and analysis pipelines.

AI.tldr: This paper improves neuron labeling in deep networks by using contrastive examples (semantically similar but low-activation inputs) alongside high-activating examples, introducing a two-stage method with VLMs for candidate label generation and CLIP-based Contrastive Semantic Projection (CSP) for label assignment, achieving more faithful and granular labels than existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing neuron labeling methods rely on highly activating examples, which often produce broad or misleading labels by focusing on dominant but incidental visual factors. The authors aim to leverage contrastive examples to sharpen explanations and produce more specific, faithful neuron-level labels.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) Candidate label generation using Vision Language Models (VLMs) with contrastive image sets (activating vs. semantically similar low-activating examples). (2) Label assignment via Contrastive Semantic Projection (CSP), an extension of SemanticLens that incorporates contrastive examples into CLIP-based scoring and selection pipeline.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments and a melanoma detection case study, contrastive labeling improves both faithfulness and semantic granularity over state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: Contrastive examples are a simple yet powerful and underutilized component for neuron labeling and analysis pipelines, offering significant improvements over existing methods.

title: Improving Driver Drowsiness Detection via Personalized EAR/MAR Thresholds and CNN-Based Classification
summary: Driver drowsiness is a major cause of traffic accidents worldwide, posing a serious threat to public safety. Vision-based driver monitoring systems often rely on fixed Eye Aspect Ratio (EAR) and Mouth Aspect Ratio (MAR) thresholds; however, such fixed values frequently fail to generalize across individuals due to variations in facial structure, illumination, and driving conditions. This paper proposes a personalized driver drowsiness detection system that monitors eyelid movements, head position, and yawning behavior in real time and provides warnings when signs of fatigue are detected. The system employs driver-specific EAR and MAR thresholds, calibrated before driving, to improve classical metric-based detection. In addition, deep learning-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models are integrated to enhance accuracy in challenging scenarios. The system is evaluated using publicly available datasets as well as a custom dataset collected under diverse lighting conditions, head poses, and user characteristics. Experimental results show that personalized thresholding improves detection accuracy by 2-3% compared to fixed thresholds, while CNN-based classification achieves 99.1% accuracy for eye state detection and 98.8% for yawning detection, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining classical metrics with deep learning for robust real-time driver monitoring.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a personalized driver drowsiness detection system that combines driver-specific EAR/MAR thresholds with CNN-based deep learning models, achieving 99.1% accuracy for eye state detection and 98.8% for yawning detection in real-time monitoring.
AI.motivation: Driver drowsiness is a major cause of traffic accidents worldwide. Existing vision-based systems rely on fixed EAR/MAR thresholds that fail to generalize across individuals due to variations in facial structure, illumination, and driving conditions.
AI.method: The system uses driver-specific EAR and MAR thresholds calibrated before driving, combined with CNN deep learning models for enhanced accuracy. It monitors eyelid movements, head position, and yawning behavior in real time.
AI.result: Personalized thresholding improves detection accuracy by 2-3% over fixed thresholds. CNN achieves 99.1% accuracy for eye state detection and 98.8% for yawning detection.
AI.conclusion: Combining classical metric-based detection with personalized thresholds and deep learning is effective for robust real-time driver drowsiness monitoring.

title: Holo360D: A Large-Scale Real-World Dataset with Continuous Trajectories for Advancing Panoramic 3D Reconstruction and Beyond
summary: While feed-forward 3D reconstruction models have advanced rapidly, they still exhibit degraded performance on panoramas due to spherical distortions. Moreover, existing panoramic 3D datasets are predominantly collected with 360 cameras fixed at discrete locations, resulting in discontinuous trajectories. These limitations critically hinder the development of panoramic feed-forward 3D reconstruction, especially for the multi-view setting. In this paper, we present Holo360D, a comprehensive dataset containing 109,495 panoramas paired with registered point clouds, meshes, and aligned camera poses. To our knowledge, Holo360D is the first large-scale dataset that provides continuous panoramic sequences with accurately aligned high-completeness depth maps. The raw data are initially collected using a 3D laser scanner coupled with a 360 camera. Subsequently, the raw data are processed with both online and offline SLAM systems. Furthermore, to enhance the 3D data quality, a post-processing pipeline tailored for the 360 dataset is proposed, including geometry denoising, mesh hole filling, and region-specific remeshing. Finally, we establish a new benchmark by fine-tuning 3D reconstruction models on Holo360D, providing key insights into effective fine-tuning strategies. Our results demonstrate that Holo360D delivers superior training signals and provides a comprehensive benchmark for advancing panoramic 3D reconstruction models. Datasets and Code will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: Holo360D is the first large-scale dataset of 109,495 continuous panoramic images with aligned point clouds, meshes, and depth maps, collected via a 3D laser scanner and 360 camera, processed with SLAM systems and a tailored post-processing pipeline, establishing a benchmark for panoramic 3D reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Existing feed-forward 3D reconstruction models perform poorly on panoramas due to spherical distortions, and current panoramic 3D datasets are limited to discrete camera locations with discontinuous trajectories, hindering multi-view panoramic 3D reconstruction development.
AI.method: Raw data collected with 3D laser scanner and 360 camera; processed with online and offline SLAM systems; followed by a post-processing pipeline including geometry denoising, mesh hole filling, and region-specific remeshing to enhance 3D data quality.
AI.result: Holo360D contains 109,495 panoramas with registered point clouds, meshes, and aligned camera poses; fine-tuning 3D reconstruction models on this dataset provides superior training signals and establishes a comprehensive benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Holo360D advances panoramic 3D reconstruction by providing continuous high-quality training data and benchmark insights; datasets and code will be publicly released.

title: CGC: Compositional Grounded Contrast for Fine-Grained Multi-Image Understanding
summary: Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have advanced rapidly, they still face notable challenges in fine-grained multi-image understanding, often exhibiting spatial hallucination, attention leakage, and failures in object constancy. In addition, existing approaches typically rely on expensive human annotations or large-scale chain-of-thought (CoT) data generation. We propose Compositional Grounded Contrast (abbr. CGC), a low-cost full framework for boosting fine-grained multi-image understanding of MLLMs. Built on existing single-image grounding annotations, CGC constructs compositional multi-image training instances through Inter-Image Contrast and Intra-Image Contrast, which introduce semantically decoupled distractor contexts for cross-image discrimination and correlated cross-view samples for object constancy, respectively. CGC further introduces a Rule-Based Spatial Reward within the GRPO framework to improve source-image attribution, spatial alignment, and structured output validity under a Think-before-Grounding paradigm. Experiments show that CGC achieves state-of-the-art results on fine-grained multi-image benchmarks, including MIG-Bench and VLM2-Bench. The learned multi-image understanding capability also transfers to broader multimodal understanding and reasoning tasks, yielding consistent gains over the Qwen3-VL-8B base model on MathVista (+2.90), MuirBench (+2.88), MMStar (+1.93), MMMU (+1.77), and BLINK (+1.69).

AI.tldr: CGC is a low-cost framework using compositional grounded contrast (inter-image and intra-image) plus a rule-based spatial reward in GRPO to boost fine-grained multi-image understanding in MLLMs, achieving SOTA on multi-image benchmarks and transferring gains to broader tasks.
AI.motivation: MLLMs struggle with fine-grained multi-image understanding (spatial hallucination, attention leakage, object constancy failures), and existing methods rely on expensive human annotations or large-scale CoT data generation.
AI.method: CGC constructs compositional multi-image training from single-image grounding annotations via (1) Inter-Image Contrast (decoupled distractors for cross-image discrimination) and (2) Intra-Image Contrast (correlated cross-view samples for object constancy), plus a Rule-Based Spatial Reward within GRPO under a Think-before-Grounding paradigm for source-image attribution, spatial alignment, and structured output validity.
AI.result: SOTA on MIG-Bench and VLM2-Bench; consistent gains over Qwen3-VL-8B base on MathVista (+2.90), MuirBench (+2.88), MMStar (+1.93), MMMU (+1.77), BLINK (+1.69).
AI.conclusion: CGC effectively and efficiently improves fine-grained multi-image understanding, and the learned capability transfers beneficially to broader multimodal understanding/reasoning tasks.

title: ICPR 2026 Competition on Low-Resolution License Plate Recognition
comment: Accepted for presentation at the International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR) 2026
summary: Low-Resolution License Plate Recognition (LRLPR) remains a challenging problem in real-world surveillance scenarios, where long capture distances, compression artifacts, and adverse imaging conditions can severely degrade license plate legibility. To promote progress in this area, we organized the ICPR 2026 Competition on Low-Resolution License Plate Recognition, the first competition specifically dedicated to LRLPR using real low-quality data collected under operationally relevant conditions. The competition was based on the LRLPR-26 dataset, which comprises 20,000 training tracks and 3,000 test tracks; each training track contains five low-resolution and five high-resolution images of the same license plate. Notably, a total of 269 teams from 41 countries registered for the competition, and 99 teams submitted valid entries in the Blind Test Phase. The winning team achieved a Recognition Rate of 82.13%, and four teams surpassed the 80% mark, highlighting both the high level of competition at the top of the leaderboard and the continued difficulty of the task. In addition to presenting the competition design, evaluation protocol, and main results, this paper summarizes the methods adopted by the top-5 teams and discusses current trends and promising directions for future research on LRLPR. The competition webpage is available at https://icpr26lrlpr.github.io/

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the ICPR 2026 Competition on Low-Resolution License Plate Recognition (LRLPR), the first dedicated competition using real low-quality data (LRLPR-26 dataset). 269 teams from 41 countries registered, with the winning team achieving 82.13% recognition accuracy. The paper details competition design, top-5 methods, and future research directions.
AI.motivation: LRLPR is challenging in real-world surveillance due to long distances, compression artifacts, and poor imaging conditions. The authors organized the first dedicated competition using real low-quality data to promote progress in this area.
AI.method: The competition used the LRLPR-26 dataset (20,000 training + 3,000 test tracks), where each track contains five low-resolution and five high-resolution images of the same plate. The competition had a Blind Test Phase with 99 valid entries evaluated by Recognition Rate.
AI.result: 269 teams from 41 countries registered; 99 valid entries. Winning team: 82.13% Recognition Rate; four teams exceeded 80%, indicating both strong competition and remaining difficulty.
AI.conclusion: The competition successfully stimulated progress in LRLPR but the top accuracy of 82.13% shows the task remains challenging. The paper summarizes top-5 methods and outlines promising future research directions.

title: Railway Artificial Intelligence Learning Benchmark (RAIL-BENCH): A Benchmark Suite for Perception in the Railway Domain
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, 5 tables, submitted at 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots & Systems
summary: Automated train operation on existing railway infrastructure requires robust camera-based perception, yet the railway domain lacks public benchmark suites with standardized evaluation protocols that would enable reproducible comparison of approaches. We present RAIL-BENCH, the first perception benchmark suite for the railway domain. It comprises five challenges - rail track detection, object detection, vegetation segmentation, multi-object tracking, and monocular visual odometry - each tailored to the specific characteristics of railway environments. RAIL-BENCH provides curated training and test datasets drawn from diverse real-world scenarios, evaluation metrics, and public scoreboards (https://www.mrt.kit.edu/railbench). For the rail track detection challenge we introduce LineAP, a novel segment-based average precision metric that evaluates the geometric accuracy of polyline predictions independently of instance-level grouping, addressing key limitations of existing line detection metrics.

AI.tldr: RAIL-BENCH is the first comprehensive perception benchmark suite for railway environments, featuring five challenges (rail track detection, object detection, vegetation segmentation, multi-object tracking, and monocular visual odometry) with standardized datasets, evaluation metrics, and a novel LineAP metric for rail track detection.
AI.motivation: Automated train operation needs robust camera-based perception, but the railway domain lacks public benchmark suites with standardized evaluation protocols for reproducible comparison of approaches.
AI.method: The authors present RAIL-BENCH with five perception challenges, curated datasets from diverse real-world scenarios, standardized evaluation metrics, public scoreboards, and introduce LineAP - a novel segment-based average precision metric for evaluating geometric accuracy of polyline predictions.
AI.result: The paper introduces the first public benchmark suite for railway perception with five challenges and a new metric (LineAP) for rail track detection, along with public datasets and scoreboards.
AI.conclusion: RAIL-BENCH fills the gap in standardized railway perception evaluation, enabling reproducible comparison of approaches through curated datasets, metrics, and public leaderboards.

title: Different Strokes for Different Folks: Writer Identification for Historical Arabic Manuscripts
summary: Handwritten Arabic manuscripts preserve the Arab world's intellectual and cultural heritage, and writer identification supports provenance, authenticity verification, and historical analysis. Using the Muharaf dataset of historical Arabic manuscripts, we evaluate writer identification from individual line images and, to the best of our knowledge, provide the first baselines reported under both line-level and page-disjoint evaluation protocols. Since the dataset is only partially labeled for writer identification, we manually verified and expanded writer labels in the public portion from 6,858 (28.00%) to 21,249 lines (86.75%) out of 24,495 line images, correcting inconsistencies and removing non-handwritten text. After further filtering, we retained 18,987 lines (77.51%). We propose a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-based model with attention mechanisms for closed-set writer identification, including rare two-writer lines modeled as composite writer-pair classes. We benchmark fourteen configurations and conduct ablations across different feature extractors and training regimes. To assess generalization to unseen pages, the page-disjoint protocol assigns all lines from each page to a single split. Under the line-level protocol, a fine-tuned DenseNet201 with attention achieves 99.05% Top-1 accuracy, 99.73% Top-5 accuracy, and 97.44% F1-score. Under the more challenging page-disjoint protocol, the best observed results are 78.61% Top-1 accuracy, 87.79% Top-5 accuracy, and 66.55% F1-score, thus quantifying the impact of page-level cues. By expanding the Muharaf dataset's labeled subset and reporting both protocols, we provide a clearer benchmark and a practical resource for historians and linguists engaged with culturally and historically significant documents. The code and implementation details are available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: This paper presents writer identification on historical Arabic manuscripts using the Muharaf dataset, expanding labels from 28% to 86.75% of lines. A CNN with attention achieves 99.05% Top-1 accuracy on line-level and 78.61% on page-disjoint protocols, providing baselines for both evaluation settings.
AI.motivation: Handwritten Arabic manuscripts preserve cultural heritage, and writer identification helps with provenance, authenticity verification, and historical analysis. The Muharaf dataset was only partially labeled, limiting research.
AI.method: The authors manually expanded writer labels in the Muharaf dataset from 6,858 to 21,249 lines, then filtered to 18,987 lines. They propose a CNN-based model with attention mechanisms for closed-set writer identification, including composite writer-pair classes for rare two-writer lines. They benchmark 14 configurations across different feature extractors and training regimes under both line-level and page-disjoint protocols.
AI.result: Under line-level: fine-tuned DenseNet201 with attention achieves 99.05% Top-1, 99.73% Top-5 accuracy, 97.44% F1. Under page-disjoint: 78.61% Top-1, 87.79% Top-5, 66.55% F1, showing page-level cue impact.
AI.conclusion: The expanded labeled subset and dual-protocol evaluation provide a clearer benchmark and practical resource for historians and linguists working with culturally significant documents.

title: Non-Minimal Sampling and Consensus for Prohibitively Large Datasets
summary: We introduce NONSAC (Non-Minimal Sampling and Consensus), a general framework for robust and scalable model estimation from arbitrarily large datasets contaminated with noise and outliers. NONSAC repeatedly samples non-minimal subsets of data and generates model hypotheses using a robust estimator, producing multiple candidate models. The final model is selected based on a predefined scoring rule that evaluates hypothesis quality. Our framework is estimator-agnostic and can be integrated with existing geometric fitting algorithms such as RANSAC to improve both scalability and robustness to outliers. We propose and evaluate various scoring rules for NONSAC on relative camera pose estimation, Perspective-n-Point, and point cloud registration. Furthermore, we showcase the applicability of NONSAC to correspondence-free point cloud registration by hypothesizing all-to-all correspondences.

AI.tldr: NONSAC is a framework that improves robust model estimation from large, noisy datasets by sampling non-minimal subsets and using a robust estimator, working with existing algorithms like RANSAC.
AI.motivation: To achieve robust and scalable model estimation from arbitrarily large datasets contaminated with noise and outliers, improving upon existing methods like RANSAC.
AI.method: Repeatedly samples non-minimal subsets of data, generates model hypotheses using a robust estimator (estimator-agnostic), and selects the final model based on a predefined scoring rule.
AI.result: Evaluated on relative camera pose estimation, Perspective-n-Point, and point cloud registration; also demonstrated for correspondence-free point cloud registration via all-to-all correspondences.
AI.conclusion: NONSAC improves scalability and robustness to outliers in geometric fitting tasks and can integrate with algorithms like RANSAC.

title: Distilling Vision Transformers for Distortion-Robust Representation Learning
summary: Self-supervised learning has achieved remarkable success in learning visual representations from clean data, yet remains challenging when clean observations are sparse or not available at all. In this paper, we demonstrate that pretrained vision models can be leveraged to learn distortion-robust representations, which can then be effectively applied to downstream tasks operating on distorted observations. In particular, we propose an asymmetric knowledge distillation framework in which both teacher and student are initialized from the same pretrained Vision Transformer but receive different views of each image: the teacher processes clean images, while the student sees their distorted versions. We introduce multi-level distillation that aligns global embeddings, patch-level features, and attention maps and show that the student is able to approximate clean-image representations despite never directly accessing clean data. We evaluate our approach on image classification tasks across several datasets and under various distortions, consistently outperforming existing alternatives for the same amount of human supervision.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an asymmetric knowledge distillation framework using pretrained Vision Transformers, where a teacher learns from clean images and a student learns from distorted versions, achieving distortion-robust representations via multi-level distillation without needing direct access to clean data.
AI.motivation: Self-supervised learning struggles when clean data is scarce or unavailable; the authors aim to learn distortion-robust representations from distorted observations without requiring clean data access during student training.
AI.method: An asymmetric knowledge distillation framework with pretrained Vision Transformer: teacher processes clean images, student processes distorted versions. Multi-level distillation aligns global embeddings, patch-level features, and attention maps between teacher and student.
AI.result: The student learns to approximate clean-image representations despite only seeing distorted data, consistently outperforming existing alternatives across multiple datasets and distortion types on image classification tasks.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained vision models can effectively teach distortion-robust representations to student models through multi-level knowledge distillation, eliminating the need for clean data access during student training while achieving strong downstream performance.

title: Evolving Thematic Map Design in Academic Cartography: A Thirty-Year Study Based on Multilingual Journals
summary: Thematic maps play a central role in academic communication, yet their large-scale design evolution has rarely been examined empirically. This study presents a longitudinal and multilingual analysis of thematic map design practices in academic cartography from 1990 to 2020. We compile a corpus of 45,732 research articles from sixteen authoritative Chinese- and English-language journals and extract 23,928 maps using computer vision and large-model-based document parsing to build a structured dataset. Map design characteristics are quantified across three dimensions: map elements, color design, and layout structure. Results show that Chinese- and Englishlanguage academic maps share highly similar structural conventions, typically employing restrained color palettes with neutral dominant hues, low saturation, high brightness, and limited hue diversity, as well as centered layouts with high main-map occupation ratios. Differences exist in that English-language maps show slightly greater hue richness and compactness, whereas Chinese-language maps historically rely more on neutral hues and integrated layouts. Temporal analysis reveals parallel evolutionary trends in both groups, including increasing element richness, legend usage, and hue diversity, alongside stable layout structures. Overall, the findings suggest that academic map design evolution is characterized more by institutional convergence than cultural divergence.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes 23,928 maps from 45,732 articles (1990-2020) across Chinese and English journals, finding that academic map design has converged institutionally rather than diverging culturally, with shared structural conventions but subtle linguistic-group differences.
AI.motivation: The large-scale empirical examination of thematic map design evolution over time and across languages has been largely missing, despite the central role of maps in academic communication.
AI.method: Compiled 45,732 articles from 16 Chinese/English journals (1990-2020); extracted 23,928 maps via computer vision and large-model-based parsing; quantified designs across three dimensions: map elements, color design, and layout structure.
AI.result: Chinese and English maps share similar conventions (restrained color palettes, neutral hues, low saturation, high brightness, centered layouts). English maps show slightly greater hue richness and compactness; Chinese maps rely more on neutral hues and integrated layouts. Temporal trends show increasing element richness, legend usage, and hue diversity in both groups.
AI.conclusion: Academic map design evolution is characterized more by institutional convergence than cultural divergence.

title: ReLIC-SGG: Relation Lattice Completion for Open-Vocabulary Scene Graph Generation
summary: Open-vocabulary scene graph generation (SGG) aims to describe visual scenes with flexible relation phrases beyond a fixed predicate set. Existing methods usually treat annotated triplets as positives and all unannotated object-pair relations as negatives. However, scene graph annotations are inherently incomplete: many valid relations are missing, and the same interaction can be described at different granularities, e.g., \textit{on}, \textit{standing on}, \textit{resting on}, and \textit{supported by}. This issue becomes more severe in open-vocabulary SGG due to the much larger relation space. We propose \textbf{ReLIC-SGG}, a relation-incompleteness-aware framework that treats unannotated relations as latent variables rather than definite negatives. ReLIC-SGG builds a semantic relation lattice to model similarity, entailment, and contradiction among open-vocabulary predicates, and uses it to infer missing positive relations from visual-language compatibility, graph context, and semantic consistency. A positive-unlabeled graph learning objective further reduces false-negative supervision, while lattice-guided decoding produces compact and semantically consistent scene graphs. Experiments on conventional, open-vocabulary, and panoptic SGG benchmarks show that ReLIC-SGG improves rare and unseen predicate recognition and better recovers missing relations.

AI.tldr: ReLIC-SGG addresses the problem of incomplete annotations in open-vocabulary scene graph generation by treating unannotated relations as latent variables instead of negatives, using a semantic relation lattice to infer missing positives and reduce false-negative supervision, achieving better rare/unseen predicate recognition.
AI.motivation: Scene graph annotations are inherently incomplete, with many valid relations missing and the same interaction describable at different granularities (e.g., on, standing on, resting on). This issue is exacerbated in open-vocabulary SGG due to the larger relation space.
AI.method: ReLIC-SGG treats unannotated relations as latent variables (not definite negatives), builds a semantic relation lattice to model similarity/entailment/contradiction among predicates, infers missing positive relations via visual-language compatibility, graph context, and semantic consistency, uses positive-unlabeled graph learning to reduce false-negative supervision, and applies lattice-guided decoding for compact, consistent scene graphs.
AI.result: Improves rare and unseen predicate recognition and better recovers missing relations on conventional, open-vocabulary, and panoptic SGG benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Relation incompleteness is a critical challenge in open-vocabulary SGG; treating missing annotations as latent positives guided by a semantic lattice effectively handles this issue and boosts performance on rare/unseen predicates.

title: Transferable Physical-World Adversarial Patches Against Pedestrian Detection Models
summary: Physical adversarial patch attacks critically threaten pedestrian detection, causing surveillance and autonomous driving systems to miss pedestrians and creating severe safety risks. Despite their effectiveness in controlled settings, existing physical attacks face two major limitations in practice: they lack systematic disruption of the multi-stage decision pipeline, enabling residual modules to offset perturbations, and they fail to model complex physical variations, leading to poor robustness. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel pedestrian adversarial patch generation method that combines multi-stage collaborative attacks with robustness enhancement under physical diversity, called TriPatch. Specifically, we design a triplet loss consisting of detection confidence suppression, bounding-box offset amplification, and non-maximum suppression (NMS) disruption, which jointly act across different stages of the detection pipeline. In addition, we introduce an appearance consistency loss to constrain the color distribution of the patch, thereby improving its adaptability under diverse imaging conditions, and incorporate data augmentation to further enhance robustness against complex physical perturbations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TriPatch achieves a higher attack success rate across multiple detector models compared to existing approaches.

AI.tldr: TriPatch is a new adversarial patch method that attacks pedestrian detection systems by combining multi-stage collaborative attacks (suppressing confidence, offsetting bounding boxes, disrupting NMS) with robustness enhancement through appearance consistency loss and data augmentation, achieving higher attack success rates across multiple detector models.
AI.motivation: Existing physical adversarial patch attacks lack systematic disruption of the multi-stage decision pipeline in detectors (allowing residual modules to offset perturbations) and fail to model complex physical variations, resulting in poor robustness in real-world settings.
AI.method: TriPatch uses a triplet loss combining detection confidence suppression, bounding-box offset amplification, and NMS disruption to jointly attack across stages, plus an appearance consistency loss for color distribution constraint and data augmentation for physical robustness.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show TriPatch achieves a higher attack success rate across multiple detector models compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: TriPatch effectively addresses limitations of prior physical adversarial attacks by systematically disrupting the entire detection pipeline and enhancing robustness to physical variations, setting a new benchmark for pedestrian adversarial patch attacks.

title: Video Analysis and Generation via a Semantic Progress Function
summary: Transformations produced by image and video generation models often evolve in a highly non-linear manner: long stretches where the content barely changes are followed by sudden, abrupt semantic jumps. To analyze and correct this behavior, we introduce a Semantic Progress Function, a one-dimensional representation that captures how the meaning of a given sequence evolves over time. For each frame, we compute distances between semantic embeddings and fit a smooth curve that reflects the cumulative semantic shift across the sequence. Departures of this curve from a straight line reveal uneven semantic pacing. Building on this insight, we propose a semantic linearization procedure that reparameterizes (or retimes) the sequence so that semantic change unfolds at a constant rate, yielding smoother and more coherent transitions. Beyond linearization, our framework provides a model-agnostic foundation for identifying temporal irregularities, comparing semantic pacing across different generators, and steering both generated and real-world video sequences toward arbitrary target pacing.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Semantic Progress Function that measures semantic change over time in generated image/video sequences, and proposes a linearization procedure to achieve constant-rate semantic pacing for smoother transitions.
AI.motivation: Generated image/video transformations exhibit non-linear semantic evolution—long periods of minimal change followed by abrupt semantic jumps—which leads to uneven and jarring transitions. The authors aim to analyze and correct this undesirable behavior.
AI.method: The method computes semantic embedding distances between frames, fits a smooth cumulative semantic shift curve, then reparameterizes the sequence so semantic change proceeds at a constant rate (linearization).
AI.result: The semantic linearization procedure produces smoother and more coherent transitions by enforcing constant-rate semantic pacing.
AI.conclusion: The framework is model-agnostic, enabling identification of temporal irregularities, comparison across generators, and steering sequences toward arbitrary target pacing.

title: Cross-Stage Coherence in Hierarchical Driving VQA: Explicit Baselines and Learned Gated Context Projectors
summary: Graph Visual Question Answering (GVQA) for autonomous driving organizes reasoning into ordered stages, namely Perception, Prediction, and Planning, where planning decisions should remain consistent with the model's own perception. We present a comparative study of cross-stage context passing on DriveLM-nuScenes using two complementary mechanisms. The explicit variant evaluates three prompt-based conditioning strategies on a domain-adapted 4B VLM (Mini-InternVL2-4B-DA-DriveLM) without additional training, reducing NLI contradiction by up to 42.6% and establishing a strong zero-training baseline. The implicit variant introduces gated context projectors, which extract a hidden-state vector from one stage and inject a normalized, gated projection into the next stage's input embeddings. These projectors are jointly trained with stage-specific QLoRA adapters on a general-purpose 8B VLM (InternVL3-8B-Instruct) while updating only approximately 0.5% of parameters. The implicit variant achieves a statistically significant 34% reduction in planning-stage NLI contradiction (bootstrap 95% CIs, p < 0.05) and increases cross-stage entailment by 50%, evaluated with a multilingual NLI classifier to account for mixed-language outputs. Planning language quality also improves (CIDEr +30.3%), but lexical overlap and structural consistency degrade due to the absence of driving-domain pretraining. Since the two variants use different base models, we present them as complementary case studies: explicit context passing provides a strong training-free baseline for surface consistency, while implicit gated projection delivers significant planning-stage semantic gains, suggesting domain adaptation as a plausible next ingredient for full-spectrum improvement.

AI.tldr: This paper compares two cross-stage context passing mechanisms for GVQA in autonomous driving. The explicit variant (training-free prompt conditioning) reduces NLI contradiction by 42.6%, while the implicit variant (gated context projectors with QLoRA, 0.5% params) achieves 34% reduction in planning-stage contradiction and 50% more cross-stage entailment, though with degraded lexical quality without domain pretraining.
AI.motivation: To ensure planning-stage decisions in autonomous driving remain logically consistent with the model's own perception and prediction outputs, addressing the need for coherent cross-stage reasoning in GVQA.
AI.method: Two mechanisms: (1) Explicit – three prompt-based conditioning strategies on an adapted 4B VLM (Mini-InternVL2-4B-DA-DriveLM) with zero training; (2) Implicit – gated context projectors extracting hidden-state vectors and injecting normalized gated projections into next-stage embeddings, jointly trained with stage-specific QLoRA adapters on an 8B VLM (InternVL3-8B-Instruct), updating only ~0.5% of parameters.
AI.result: Explicit variant reduces NLI contradiction by 42.6%. Implicit variant achieves 34% reduction in planning-stage NLI contradiction (bootstrap 95% CIs, p<0.05), 50% increase in cross-stage entailment, and CIDEr +30.3% improvement in planning language quality. However, lexical overlap and structural consistency degrade due to no driving-domain pretraining.
AI.conclusion: The two variants are complementary case studies: explicit context passing offers a strong training-free baseline for surface consistency, while implicit gated projection provides significant semantic gains in planning, with domain adaptation suggested as a next step for full-spectrum improvement.

title: FlowAnchor: Stabilizing the Editing Signal for Inversion-Free Video Editing
summary: We propose FlowAnchor, a training-free framework for stable and efficient inversion-free, flow-based video editing. Inversion-free editing methods have recently shown impressive efficiency and structure preservation in images by directly steering the sampling trajectory with an editing signal. However, extending this paradigm to videos remains challenging, often failing in multi-object scenes or with increased frame counts. We identify the root cause as the instability of the editing signal in high-dimensional video latent spaces, which arises from imprecise spatial localization and length-induced magnitude attenuation. To overcome this challenge, FlowAnchor explicitly anchors both where to edit and how strongly to edit. It introduces Spatial-aware Attention Refinement, which enforces consistent alignment between textual guidance and spatial regions, and Adaptive Magnitude Modulation, which adaptively preserves sufficient editing strength. Together, these mechanisms stabilize the editing signal and guide the flow-based evolution toward the desired target distribution. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FlowAnchor achieves more faithful, temporally coherent, and computationally efficient video editing across challenging multi-object and fast-motion scenarios. The project page is available at https://cuc-mipg.github.io/FlowAnchor.github.io/.

AI.tldr: FlowAnchor is a training-free, inversion-free framework for stable flow-based video editing that uses Spatial-aware Attention Refinement and Adaptive Magnitude Modulation to stabilize editing signals in high-dimensional video latent spaces, achieving faithful and temporally coherent results.
AI.motivation: Current inversion-free editing methods work well for images but fail in video domains, especially for multi-object scenes or longer frame sequences, due to instability of editing signals caused by imprecise spatial localization and length-induced magnitude attenuation in high-dimensional video latent spaces.
AI.method: FlowAnchor introduces two key mechanisms: (1) Spatial-aware Attention Refinement for consistent alignment between textual guidance and spatial regions, and (2) Adaptive Magnitude Modulation for adaptively preserving sufficient editing strength, both stabilizing the editing signal in flow-based video editing.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show FlowAnchor achieves more faithful, temporally coherent, and computationally efficient video editing, particularly in challenging multi-object and fast-motion scenarios.
AI.conclusion: FlowAnchor effectively addresses the instability of inversion-free video editing by anchoring both where and how strongly to edit, enabling stable and efficient performance without additional training.

title: EV-CLIP: Efficient Visual Prompt Adaptation for CLIP in Few-shot Action Recognition under Visual Challenges
summary: CLIP has demonstrated strong generalization in visual domains through natural language supervision, even for video action recognition. However, most existing approaches that adapt CLIP for action recognition have primarily focused on temporal modeling, often overlooking spatial perception. In real-world scenarios, visual challenges such as low-light environments or egocentric viewpoints can severely impair spatial understanding, an essential precursor for effective temporal reasoning. To address this limitation, we propose Efficient Visual Prompting for CLIP (EV-CLIP), an efficient adaptation framework designed for few-shot video action recognition across diverse scenes and viewpoints. EV-CLIP introduces two visual prompts: mask prompts, which guide the model's attention to action-relevant regions by reweighting pixels, and context prompts, which perform lightweight temporal modeling by compressing frame-wise features into a compact representation. For a comprehensive evaluation, we curate five benchmark datasets and analyze domain shifts to quantify the influence of diverse visual and semantic factors on action recognition. Experimental results demonstrate that EV-CLIP outperforms existing parameter-efficient methods in overall performance. Moreover, its efficiency remains independent of the backbone scale, making it well-suited for deployment in real-world, resource-constrained scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/AI-CV-Lab/EV-CLIP.

AI.tldr: EV-CLIP introduces efficient visual prompting (mask prompts for spatial attention and context prompts for lightweight temporal modeling) to adapt CLIP for few-shot video action recognition, outperforming parameter-efficient methods across diverse scenes and viewpoints.
AI.motivation: Existing CLIP adaptations for action recognition focus heavily on temporal modeling while overlooking spatial perception, which is critical in challenging real-world scenarios (low-light, egocentric views).
AI.method: EV-CLIP proposes two visual prompts: (1) mask prompts that reweight pixels to guide attention to action-relevant regions, and (2) context prompts that compress frame-wise features for lightweight temporal modeling.
AI.result: Outperforms existing parameter-efficient methods on five curated benchmark datasets; efficiency is independent of backbone scale, suitable for resource-constrained deployment.
AI.conclusion: EV-CLIP effectively balances spatial and temporal adaptation for CLIP in few-shot video action recognition, demonstrating robustness across domain shifts and practical efficiency for real-world deployment.

title: A Non-Invasive Alternative to RFID: Self-Sufficient 3D Identification of Group-Housed Livestock
summary: Accurate identification of individual farm animals in group-housed environments is a cornerstone of precision livestock management. However, current industry standards rely heavily on Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) ear tags, which are invasive, prone to loss, and restricted by the spatial limitations of antenna fields. In this paper, we propose a non-intrusive, vision-based identification system leveraging 3D point cloud data captured within a commercial electronic feeding station (EFS). Departing from traditional supervised frame-level inference, we introduce the Temporal Adaptive Recognition Architecture (TARA), a self-sufficient, semi-supervised framework designed to maintain identity consistency over time. TARA employs a dynamic recalibration mechanism that updates individual identity profiles to account for morphological changes in the livestock. To facilitate training in label-scarce environments, we utilize a visit-level majority voting strategy to generate high-fidelity pseudo-labels from raw temporal sequences. Experimental results on a group housed sow dataset collected from an operational commercial barn demonstrate that our approach achieves 100% identification accuracy at the visit level. These results suggest that vision-based 3D point cloud analysis offers a robust, superior alternative to RFID-based systems, paving the way for fully autonomous individual animal monitoring.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes TARA, a semi-supervised vision-based framework using 3D point cloud data from electronic feeding stations to identify individual farm animals. It achieves 100% visit-level identification accuracy, outperforming traditional RFID ear tags.
AI.motivation: Current livestock identification relies on RFID ear tags, which are invasive, prone to loss, and limited by antenna spatial range. There is a need for a non-intrusive, accurate alternative.
AI.method: TARA (Temporal Adaptive Recognition Architecture) is a semi-supervised framework that uses 3D point cloud data from commercial EFS. It employs dynamic recalibration to update identity profiles, and a visit-level majority voting strategy to generate high-fidelity pseudo-labels for training in label-scarce environments.
AI.result: The approach achieves 100% identification accuracy at the visit level on a group-housed sow dataset from an operational commercial barn.
AI.conclusion: Vision-based 3D point cloud analysis is a robust, superior alternative to RFID-based systems, enabling fully autonomous individual animal monitoring.

title: PASR: Pose-Aware 3D Shape Retrieval from Occluded Single Views
summary: Single-view 3D shape retrieval is a fundamental yet challenging task that is increasingly important with the growth of available 3D data. Existing approaches largely fall into two categories: those using contrastive learning to map point cloud features into existing vision-language spaces and those that learn a common embedding space for 2D images and 3D shapes. However, these feed-forward, holistic alignments are often difficult to interpret, which in turn limits their robustness and generalization to real-world applications. To address this problem, we propose Pose-Aware 3D Shape Retrieval (PASR), a framework that formulates retrieval as a feature-level analysis-by-synthesis problem by distilling knowledge from a 2D foundation model (DINOv3) into a 3D encoder. By aligning pose-conditioned 3D projections with 2D feature maps, our method bridges the gap between real-world images and synthetic meshes. During inference, PASR performs a test-time optimization via analysis-by-synthesis, jointly searching for the shape and pose that best reconstruct the patch-level feature map of the input image. This synthesis-based optimization is inherently robust to partial occlusion and sensitive to fine-grained geometric details. PASR substantially outperforms existing methods on both clean and occluded 3D shape retrieval datasets by a wide margin. Additionally, PASR demonstrates strong multi-task capabilities, achieving robust shape retrieval, competitive pose estimation, and accurate category classification within a single framework.

AI.tldr: PASR introduces a pose-aware 3D shape retrieval framework that uses analysis-by-synthesis with a 2D foundation model (DINOv3) to align pose-conditioned 3D projections with 2D features, achieving state-of-the-art performance on both clean and occluded datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing methods use feed-forward holistic alignment (contrastive learning or shared embedding spaces) which are difficult to interpret, limiting robustness and generalization to real-world applications.
AI.method: Distills knowledge from a 2D foundation model (DINOv3) into a 3D encoder, aligns pose-conditioned 3D projections with 2D feature maps, and performs test-time optimization via analysis-by-synthesis to jointly search for shape and pose that reconstruct the input image's patch-level feature map.
AI.result: Substantially outperforms existing methods on clean and occluded 3D shape retrieval datasets by a wide margin; demonstrates robust shape retrieval, competitive pose estimation, and accurate category classification within a single framework.
AI.conclusion: The synthesis-based optimization is inherently robust to partial occlusion and sensitive to fine-grained geometric details, making PASR effective for real-world applications.

title: SS3D: End2End Self-Supervised 3D from Web Videos
summary: We present SS3D, a web-scale SfM-based self-supervision pretraining pipeline for feed-forward 3D estimation from monocular video. Our model jointly predicts depth, ego-motion, and intrinsics in a single forward pass and is trained/evaluated as a coherent end-to-end 3D estimator. To stabilize joint learning, we use an intrinsics-first two-stage schedule and a unified single-checkpoint evaluation protocol. Scaling SfM self-supervision to unconstrained web video is challenging due to weak multi-view observability and strong corpus heterogeneity; we address these with a multi-view signal proxy (MVS) used for filtering and curriculum sampling, and with expert training distilled into a single student. Pretraining on YouTube-8M (~100M frames after filtering) yields strong cross-domain zero-shot transfer and improved fine-tuning performance over prior self-supervised baselines. We release the pretrained checkpoint and code.

AI.tldr: SS3D is a web-scale self-supervision pipeline that uses SfM to pretrain a feed-forward model for joint depth, ego-motion, and intrinsics estimation from monocular video, achieving strong zero-shot transfer by pretraining on ~100M YouTube-8M frames.
AI.motivation: To scale SfM-based self-supervision to unconstrained web video, addressing challenges of weak multi-view observability and strong corpus heterogeneity in web-scale data.
AI.method: (1) Intrinsics-first two-stage training schedule for stable joint learning. (2) Multi-view signal proxy (MVS) for filtering and curriculum sampling. (3) Expert training distilled into a single student. (4) Unified single-checkpoint evaluation protocol.
AI.result: Pretraining on YouTube-8M (~100M frames) yields strong cross-domain zero-shot transfer and improved fine-tuning over prior self-supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: Web-scale SfM self-supervision is feasible and effective for monocular 3D estimation; checkpoints and code are released.

title: Generative Modeling of Neurodegenerative Brain Anatomy with 4D Longitudinal Diffusion Model
summary: Understanding and predicting the progression of neurodegenerative diseases remains a major challenge in medical AI, with significant implications for early diagnosis, disease monitoring, and treatment planning. However, most available longitudinal neuroimaging datasets are temporally sparse with a few follow-up scans per subject. This scarcity of temporal data limits our ability to model and accurately capture the continuous anatomical changes related to disease progression in individual subjects. To address this problem, we propose a novel 4D (3DxT) diffusion-based generative framework that effectively models and synthesizes longitudinal brain anatomy over time, conditioned on available clinical variables such as health status, age, sex, and other relevant factors. Moreover, while most current approaches focus on manipulating image intensity or texture, our method explicitly learns the data distribution of topology-preserving spatiotemporal deformations to effectively capture the geometric changes of brain structures over time. This design enables the realistic generation of future anatomical states and the reconstruction of anatomically consistent disease trajectories, providing a more faithful representation of longitudinal brain changes. We validate our model through both synthetic sequence generation and downstream longitudinal disease classification, as well as brain segmentation. Experiments on two large-scale longitudinal neuroimage datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in generating anatomically accurate, temporally consistent, and clinically meaningful brain trajectories. Our code is available on Github.

AI.tldr: A novel 4D diffusion-based generative model that synthesizes longitudinal brain anatomy over time by learning topology-preserving spatiotemporal deformations, outperforming baselines in generating anatomically accurate and clinically meaningful brain trajectories for neurodegenerative disease analysis.
